label: list_item

label: text
text_preview: UNESCO and UNEP jointly organized first intergovernmental conference on environmental education in 1977 at Tbilisi, Georgia. The goals of the conference were:

label: list_item
text_preview:  To foster clear awareness of, and concern about, economic, social, political, and ecological interdependence in urban and rural areas;

label: list_item
text_preview:  To provide every person with opportunities to acquire the knowledge, values, attitudes, commitment, and skills needed to protect and improve the environment;

label: list_item
text_preview:  To create new patterns of behavior of individuals, groups, and society as a whole towards the environment.

label: text
text_preview: The categories of environmental education objectives are:

label: list_item
text_preview:  Awareness -to help social groups and individuals acquire an awareness and sensitivity to the total environment and its allied problems.

label: list_item
text_preview:  Knowledge -to help social groups and individuals gain a variety of experience in, and acquire a basic understanding of, the environment and its associated problems.

label: list_item
text_preview:  Attitudes -to help social groups and individuals acquire a set of values and feelings of concern for the environment and the motivation for actively participating in environmental improvement and protection.

label: list_item
text_preview:  Skills -to help social groups and individuals acquire the skills for identifying and solving environmental problems.

label: list_item
text_preview:  Participation -to provide social groups and individuals with an opportunity to be actively involved at all levels in working toward resolution of environmental problems.

label: text
text_preview: Ecosystem is the basic structural and functional unit of the environment. Both the living and non-living component of the nature, when interact with each other to establish a stable living community, it is called as Ecosystem. That means there is a c...

label: text
text_preview: The study of ecosystem includes complete analysis of the structure, regulation and the role of each and every component functioning there. Once we know it, in detail, the study of the ecosystem and its importance to the equilibrium of environment wil...

label: section_header
text_preview: 1. DEFINITION OF ECOSYSTEM

label: text
text_preview: The study of all the physical as well as biological processes including the distribution and abundance of living organisms and the interaction in between them with their surrounding environment is known as Ecosystem. In simpler words, if any environm...

label: text
text_preview: The ecosystem is very complex in nature and human beings are a part of the ecosystems also.

label: text
text_preview: The theme of the ecosystem is 'energy flow'. Sustenance of ecosystem is possible due to the energy dependence as well as energy transfer between various components of an ecosystem may it be living or non-living.

label: text
text_preview: Examples of Ecosystems: Terrestrial or land-based ecosystem, Aquatic or water-based ecosystems etc.

label: section_header
text_preview: 2.1. BIOTIC COMPONENTS

label: text
text_preview: Biotic components are also divided into 3 categories basing upon their food-fed relationships (Fig.2).

label: list_item
text_preview: 2.1.1. PRODUCERS : Producers are the autotrophs (auto means self, troph means to nourish) of the ecosystem. They are the green plants and green microorganisms who can make their own food material by using carbon dioxide and water in presence of sunli...

label: text
text_preview: Ex. All green plants, Algae, Cyanobacteria,

label: list_item
text_preview: 2.1.2. CONSUMERS : Consumers are the heterotrophs (hetero-not self troph to nourish) of an ecosystem. They depend on the producers of the nature to get energy. Consumers are also different types like

label: list_item
text_preview: Primary Consumers - They are the herbivores who eat directly the autotrophs / plants. They cannot eat any animals.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Grasshoppers, Rabbits, Goats.

label: list_item
text_preview: Secondary Consumers -They cannot directly eat the producers of the ecosystem, that is the plants. They can eat only herbivores.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Frogs, Jackal, Snakes

label: list_item
text_preview: Tertiary Consumers - They are carnivores in nature means they are the meat eaters. Thus, they depend on the secondary consumer for their food. They are the top-level carnivores.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. - Tigers, Lions, Vulture, Kite

label: text

label: section_header
text_preview: 2.2. ABIOTIC COMPONENTS:

label: text
text_preview: These are the non-living factors in form of solid, liquid or gas found in the nature (ice, water, moisture). They can be categorised into 2 types.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Light, Temperature, Humidity, Rain etc.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Soil, Organic and Inorganic components of the soil, Substratum etc.

label: text
text_preview: Let's discuss importance of each abiotic factors and their limitations in an ecosystem.

label: section_header
text_preview: 2.2.1. CLIMATIC FACTORS

label: list_item
text_preview: 3.2.1.1. Light - It is an essential factor for all the living organisms like producers, consumers as well as decomposers. In presence of the sunlight plants are able to prepare their food material, which in turn eaten by the heterotrophs & ultimately...

label: list_item
text_preview: Importance of light for plants

label: list_item
text_preview: Chlorophyll production

label: list_item
text_preview: Distribution of plants: The vegetation or the types of plants grow on the earth depends on the amount of sunlight they are getting. Thus the vegetation of temperate, tropical as well as Tundra regions are different from each other.

label: list_item
text_preview: Light thus decides the physiology of the plants of different regions.

label: list_item
text_preview: Temperature: When light increases, temperature also increase and vice versa. When temperature increases rate of transpiration in the plants increases. Thus, the absorption of water from the underground also increases. It clearly explains that temp al...

label: list_item
text_preview: Stomatal Movement: The stomata are present in the leaves of the plants. They control the evaporation of water from a plant body. Thus, by opening and closing, stomata keeps the plant body in a stable physiological status.

label: list_item
text_preview: Duration of Light: During summer and winter months the type of flowers, what we can observe in the nature are different. The basic theory behind it is some plants can bloom in summer months are long day (basing on the exposure to the sun light) plant...

label: list_item
text_preview: ii) Importance of Light for animals

label: list_item
text_preview: Metabolism: Light controls the physiology & metabolism of animals by affecting it's enzymatic activity.

label: list_item
text_preview: Vision: Without light it is very difficult to see anything. So it is required by all animals.

label: list_item
text_preview: Pigmentation: The process of pigmentation on the skin color depends on the natural light source.

label: list_item
text_preview: Reproduction: Different animals respond to different duration of light exposure for their breeding activities.

label: list_item
text_preview: Circadian Rhythm: Daily response of the animals towards the light condition are known as circadian rhythm. This is an important physiological action of all the living organisms. Thus, it is a light dependent process.

label: text
text_preview: 3.2.1.2 Temperature : Temperature affects animals and plants in following ways.

label: list_item
text_preview: Importance of temperature for plants

label: list_item
text_preview: Metabolism: Temperature increase the physiological activity. Thus, the types of plants grow in a desert ecosystem are different from the type of plants of a forest ecosystem and an aquatic ecosystem.

label: list_item
text_preview: On Growth & Development: Different types of plants need different range of temperatures for their growth. Summer growing plants are different from winter season growing plants. This is self-explanatory.

label: list_item
text_preview: Thermal Stratification: Best example is an aquatic ecosystem. The organism growing at surface layer are different as they need more temperature and light than the organisms grow at a deeper level in an aquatic ecosystem.

label: list_item
text_preview: ii) Importance of Temperature for Animals

label: text
text_preview: Basing on need of the temperature, animals are categorised into two types. Like:

label: list_item
text_preview: Warm-Blooded or Endo- thermic animals

label: list_item
text_preview: Cold - Blooded or Ecto-thermic Animals

label: text
text_preview: Warm blooded animals maintain their body temperature at a constant level. Their body temperature does not change with the environment temperature.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Mammals, Birds, etc.

label: text
text_preview: The body temperature of the organism when changes with the temperature of the environment, then they are known as Cold Blooded animals.

label: list_item
text_preview: Migration: Temperature is also a factor for migration of animals. It is known as thermal migration. If temperature changes the locomotory animals or birds may change their places for a temporary period & them comeback to their original ecosystem when...

label: text
text_preview: 3.2.1.3. Water : Life is never possible without water. So, water is a very important abiotic factor in an ecosystem. The amount of water present in an area decides the type of ecosystem to be developed there. In this regard sometimes aquatic ecosyste...

label: text
text_preview: Beside for photosynthesis & other metabolic activity plants needs water for circulation of minerals throughout its body.

label: text
text_preview: Water is also an essential requirement of the animals for their metabolic activity.

label: text
text_preview: Water regulates the body temperature of both plants and animals.

label: text
text_preview: Water in forms of rainfall also needed by the ecosystem. It maintains the humidity & content of moisture in the atmosphere. Amount of rainfall also decides the kind of plants to grow over there.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Deciduous forest, Evergreen forest, Deserts etc.

label: text
text_preview: With the type of vegetation, different types of animals also started living in that particular geographic area.

label: text
text_preview: Water in form of humidity also affects plants and animal lives. Some plants can grow in less humid area where some other plants need more moisture in their environment. Some plants can use atmospheric moisture directly from the environment.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Epiphytes, Orchids, Lichens, Mosses etc.

label: text
text_preview: Some other needs it in liquid form from underground by the process of absorption. Ex. Higher plants.

label: section_header
text_preview: 2.2.2. EDAPHIC FACTORS

label: text
text_preview: 3.2.2.1. Soil : Soil is a natural resource and provides platform for the plants to grow & animals to dwell on it. Thus, the fertility and quantity of soil decides the type of vegetation in that area. To be more specific, pH of the soil is the decidin...

label: caption
text_preview: Fig.3: Schematic representation of different texture of the soil

label: text
text_preview: (Ref:https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/45/Horizons.gif)

label: text
text_preview: Thus, the acidity & alkalinity of the soil decides the number & kind of plants & animals to stay over there (Fig.3). But needless to say, neutral soil, i.e. when the pH ranges from 6.5 -7.5 is the preferred condition for most of the living organism.

label: text
text_preview: Soil is also associated with different kinds of minerals & nutrients. Some are required in more quantity, are called macronutrient and some are micronutrients which are required in a small quantity. They also play a vital role in development and sust...

label: text
text_preview: Texture of the soil is also playing a key role. For ex. Rocky areas, Swampy areas, Mining areas etc.

label: text
text_preview: Different textured soil will allow different types of biotic factors or organisms to grow and establish in that ecosystem.

label: section_header
text_preview: 3. ECOLOGICAL PYRAMIDS

label: text

label: text
text_preview: Definition: An ecological pyramid is a graphical representation of the relationship between various trophic levels organisms in a Food Chain.

label: text
text_preview: It was first designed by Charles Elton and are called Eltonian Pyramid or Food Pyramids.

label: text
text_preview: Ecological Pyramids are three types:

label: section_header
text_preview: 1. Pyramid of Number

label: section_header
text_preview: A. Pyramid of Numbers:

label: text
text_preview: As the name says, in this type of pyramid number of organisms are counted and accordingly respective volume to each trophic level is allotted in the pyramid. Characteristic features of pyramid of number are.

label: list_item
text_preview: Number of producers are higher in number than the consumers present in other trophic levels.

label: list_item
text_preview: Number of individuals in each trophic level decrease when we go from bottom to top in a pyramid.

label: list_item
text_preview: Collectively the living organisms present in each of the trophic levels are known as standing crop.

label: list_item
text_preview: Normally the pyramid of number is an upright (Fig.5) one Ex. Grassland ecosystem, Aquatic ecosystem.

label: list_item
text_preview: Sometimes inverted pyramids (Fig.6) are also found in this case.

label: list_item
text_preview: Amixed or spindle shaped pyramid is also observed in case of forest ecosystem.

label: list_item
text_preview: Pyramid of Biomass: Biomass is the total dry weight of the animals as well as plants present in the ecosystem at any point of time.

label: text
text_preview: Like pyramid of numbers it may be upright one or inverted one. In case of a forest and grass land ecosystem it is upright and in cases of a pond ecosystem inverted pyramids (Fig.7) are observed.

label: text
text_preview: Some limitations are also observed in the studies of ecological pyramids. That can be described as follows:

label: list_item
text_preview: The position of certain organisms are not fixed in one ecological pyramid. That may vary in same or may be in a different ecosystem.

label: list_item
text_preview: There is no consideration of seasonal changes, while studying the pyramids

label: list_item
text_preview: Role of detrivores are not represented in an ecological pyramid.

label: text
text_preview: At last it can be said that the pyramid of energy is very accurate and gives a true picture of the ecosystem.

text_preview: Definition: The transfer of food energy from the source in plants through a series of organisms with repeated eating & being eaten is referred to or called as food chain (Odum, 1971).

label: text
text_preview: How Food chain works:

label: text
text_preview: The green plants are the autotrophs or the producer of the ecosystem. With the help of chlorophyll, and by using CO2 and H2O from nature in presence of sunlight the plants can prepare their own food known as carbohydrates. The process is known as pho...

label: text
text_preview: That's why plants are called autotrophs (auto-self; troph-to nourish)

label: text
text_preview: Thus, plants are the first step in a food chain.

label: text
text_preview: The heterotrophs (hetero= other; troph = to nourish) are the organisms who depends on the autotrophs for food and energy.

label: text
text_preview: Heterotrophs are two types - Herbivores and Carnivores

label: text
text_preview: Herbivores are the herb/plant eaters. They can only eat the green plants/autotrophs.

label: text
text_preview: Ex. Grasshoppers, Rabbits, Goats etc.

label: text
text_preview: Carnivores are the meat eaters in an ecosystem. They cannot have the ability to eat herbs.

label: text
text_preview: For example, Frogs, Snakes, Hawks, Lions, Tigers. Thus, in an ecosystem a chain like structure is found based on '-to eat and being eaten 'scheme, which is also called 'PrayPredator relationship' in an ecosystem (Fig.8).

text_preview: TYPES OF ECOSYSTEMS AND ECOSYSTEMSERVICES

label: text
text_preview: Ecosystems can range from small water bodies to an ocean, similarly on land from a patch of woods to a forest. Ecosystems can be natural or anthropogenic (human-created like farmlands). Broadly Ecosystem is classified into terrestrial and aquatic eco...

label: text
text_preview: Ecosystem sustains mankind on earth by providing various products and services. There are several kinds of ecosystem services which maintain the basic functioning of the planet.

text_preview: Due to the exploitation of resources by ever-growing human population ecosystems are getting degraded. Therefore it is essential to understand various concepts of preservation, conservation and restoration of ecosystem.

text_preview: 3. Ecosystem preservation and conservation strategies

label: text
text_preview: Ecological restoration: It is a process of assisting the recovery of an ecosystem that has been partially or completely degraded. In simple words ecological restoration means restoring the ecosystem, to a former state or to an perfect condition. Ecol...

label: list_item
text_preview:  Health of the ecosystem: purification of air, sequestration of carbon dioxide, filtrationof water

label: list_item
text_preview:  Integrity of ecosystem: Species composition and community structure

label: list_item
text_preview:  Sustainability: Resistance and resilience to disturbance

label: text
text_preview: Rehabilitation: It mean returning of a degraded land to a fully functional ecosystem irrespective of its original state but according to a prior landuse plan. Examples of the rehabilitation process are partial recovery of species diversity and ecolog...

label: text
text_preview: Remediation: It is a process in which using physical and biological methods, chemical contaminants are cleaned from polluted ecosystem in order to protect human and ecosystem health.

label: text
text_preview: Reclamation: It is a process by which biotic function and productivity of severely damaged land is restored.

label: text
text_preview: Mitigation: It is defined as restoration, rehabilitation or reclamation process to reduce the effect of the source of degradation.

label: section_header
text_preview: Conservation strategies

label: section_header
text_preview: In-situ conservation

label: text
text_preview: It includes methods to conserve species in place of their natural habitat through legal means. In-situ conservation can be divided into following three types:

label: list_item
text_preview: (i) National Park - National Parks are protected areas in which complete legal protection is provided to ecosystem. Therefore, biotic components as well as abiotic components are protected in these areas through legal means.

label: text
text_preview: National Parks are created on the basis of core-buffer strategy. Core area is central region of national park which is completely prohibited from human activities. Buffer zone is peripheral area of National Park which allows limited tourism.

label: text
text_preview: Example -Jim Corbett National Park

label: text
text_preview: (Uttarakhand) Rajaji National

label: list_item
text_preview: (ii) Wildlife Sanctuary - Wildlife sanctuary is protected area which is created by government in order to conserve a particular species which may be of local, national or international significance.

label: text
text_preview: Wildlife sanctuaries are also created on the basis on core-buffer strategy. Core area is central region of wildlife sanctuary which is completely prohibited from human activities. Buffer region is peripheral region in which activities like tourism, f...

label: text
text_preview: Example - Chilka Bird Sanctuary (Odisha)

label: list_item
text_preview: (iii) Biosphere Reserves - These protected areas are based on the concept of including humans in conservation efforts. This concept was formulated during Man and Biosphere (MAB) programme of UNESCO.

label: text
text_preview: Biosphere reserves are protected areas conserve biodiversity and provide livelihood to local communities. Multiple activities like forestry, agriculture, tourism etc. are permitted in peripheral region of Biosphere Reserve.

label: text
text_preview: ExampleNandadevi Biosphere Reserve

label: text
text_preview: (Uttarakhand) Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve

label: section_header
text_preview: Ex-situ Conservation

label: text
text_preview: In this method, plant or animal species are protected in a place which is outside their natural habitat. Ex-situ conservation is done by following methods:

label: list_item
text_preview: (i) Z oo - These are places in which species are kept in artificial enclosures. Zoo serves purpose of educating citizens about wildlife. Various animal breeding programs are alsocarried out in zoo to increase population of species.

label: list_item
text_preview: (ii) Gene Bank - Genetic material of any species provides valuable details of its existence. Gene banks are those institutes in which genes of different species are preserved for future use.

label: list_item
text_preview: (iii) Seed bank - Some Institutes are involved in preserving seeds of valuable plant species. These seeds may be used in future, if any plant species becomes extinct.

label: text
text_preview: IARI (Indian Agricultural Research Institute) is seed bank located in Delhi.
